why: Several content signals point to the structured multi-agent panel rather than a single assistant (bare or self-deliberating). First, the assumptions list separates a "decision-maker" role from the analysis itself ("The decision-maker has full authority to approve this decision without further review") — this only makes sense as an explicit assumption if the artifact-producing process distinguishes advisors/analysis from an accountable decider role, matching Arm C's architecture (advisors -> accountable decider -> records process) rather than a single assistant reasoning about its own authority. Second, the assumption "Manually verifying the record against the applicable formatting and content rules is an adequate substitute, given that the automated verification tool could not be run in this session" describes a distinct records/verification step checking the artifact against formatting rules via an automated tool — this maps directly onto a records-and-verify pipeline (e.g., an ADR-lint-style gate) that is a structural component of a multi-agent decision process, not something a bare or self-critiquing single assistant would naturally produce as a listed assumption. Third, the options are framed as fully-formed alternatives each with embedded rejection rationale citing specific prior quantitative facts (25% false-accept rate, an earlier spending-before-checking rule) in a consistent, panel-like "options considered and rejected" format rather than a single train of self-argument. Together these suggest a role-differentiated, process-with-verification origin — i.e., Arm C — rather than Arm A (too much structured deliberation and explicit process-role awareness for a bare ask-and-decide) or Arm B (the decider/verifier role separation and tool-based record verification go beyond what a single self-arguing assistant would typically surface as assumptions).

The artifact's shape matches this repo's own pdca-workflow ADR convention almost exactly (decision / options-with-rejections / criterion-to-reopen / risks / assumptions), which in CLAUDE.md is explicitly produced by the advise -> PM-decides -> ADR -> verify(fresh-eyes+red-team) pipeline — i.e. Arm C's advisors/decider/records-process structure, not a single model's free-form answer. Several specific tells point to a distinct verify/red-team pass rather than one continuous stream of self-argument: the risks list reads like adversarial red-teaming framed in third person about the decision itself (\"discretionary judgments are exploitable in both directions,\" \"source material's own language... biases toward preferring that option,\" \"treated as a survivorship-bias trap\") rather than a single author's live hedging; the criterion field encodes exact script thresholds (4 non-tied pairs, -0.05 fail-under, 0.7x ratio) as a reopen-trigger contract, which is the ADR \"reopen if\" convention, not a natural output of a bare Q&A or even a single deliberating assistant. The one place a single-agent voice does show through is the assumptions' confession that the environment blocked live script execution with arguments so a passing unit test was relied on instead, flagged explicitly as \"weakest assumption\" — but this reads as an advisor's evidence note carried into the decider's record rather than the decider's own live struggle, consistent with a panel process compiling advisor findings into a final structured record. Given the strong structural match to this repo's named multi-stage decision pipeline (advise -> decide -> record -> verify) and the red-team-style risk framing, I lean C, though I can't fully rule out Arm B producing similarly elaborate self-critique under a large token budget.

The artifact's shape matches a structured panel + accountable-decider + records process rather than a single model's monologue (even a self-arguing one). Three tells: (1) The options are written as formal "REJECTED: ..." verdicts with terse, non-overlapping rationale for each — reads like a decider synthesizing distinct advisor positions (an "adopt the framework" advocate, a "do nothing" advocate) into a record, not one voice narrating its own back-and-forth. (2) The risks section functions as an independent red-team/audit pass on the decision's own evidentiary claims — e.g. catching that the cited "14 months zero failures" claim rests on a 30-day log that is "structurally incapable of ever recording a failure," and that the "pages the team on failure" claim doesn't hold because "the script contains no alerting code... not verified." That's the signature of a distinct verification role checking another party's assertions, not the same assistant hedging its own draft. (3) The criterion section sets multiple independently-sourced quantified thresholds (job count, failure-rate %, backfill cadence, heartbeat-miss frequency) spanning different domains (scaling, reliability, ops cadence) — consistent with several advisors each contributing their domain's trigger condition, then a decider merging them into one criterion, plus an explicit note that reopening picks a framework only "as a separate decision" (a records-process instinct to keep decisions atomic). This also matches the calling repo's own dogfooded pdca-workflow (decide -> ADR -> fresh-eyes + red-team verify), which produces exactly this decision/options/risks/assumptions/criterion record shape — strong circumstantial support for Arm C over a bare or self-arguing single-assistant run.

The artifact's shape matches the repo's own pdca-workflow /decide process described in CLAUDE.md (advise -> PM decides -> ADR in docs/decisions -> verify), not a single free-form rationale. Specifically: (1) the "options" field enumerates the adopted choice plus two explicitly REJECTED alternatives, each with advisor-style tradeoff reasoning ("would just be a second prose rule with a permission dependency, not additional mechanization" / "there is no backlog-dispatch or orchestration script... confirmed by searching") — this reads like a decider synthesizing and adjudicating advisor proposals, not a lone model second-guessing itself. (2) The presence of a formal "criterion" (revisit trigger) and separate "risks"/"assumptions" fields mirrors the canonical ADR record fields (rationale, rejected alternatives, revisit triggers) that the repo's decision-log process mandates. (3) The self-referential process risk noted ("the lint/check tool... was blocked by sandbox approval... hand-traced against the actual files instead of being run by the tool itself; the real command should be re-run before merge") reads like a records/verification step flagging a gap for a later fresh-eyes/red-team check — consistent with Arm C's built-in verify stage — rather than a single bare-decide response (Arm A, which wouldn't produce this much structured alternative-analysis) or a single self-arguing model (Arm B, which typically produces a linear for/against monologue rather than a cleanly separated options-with-rejections-plus-criterion ADR schema). The specificity to this repo's actual ADR 0043/character-budget conventions further suggests the multi-step structured panel process rather than an ad hoc single-pass output.

The artifact has the texture of a single coherent voice that argued against itself, not a multi-agent aggregation. Signals: (1) the rejected option's rationale explicitly raises and then rebuts the strongest counterargument ("adopting it in anticipation of future growth is not justified, since the same migration would cost the same later, once the need is actually real rather than speculative") — this is a self-directed steelman-then-rebut move, the fingerprint of an "argue against yourself" instruction, not a simple rationale. (2) The revisit criterion is compound and carefully scoped (job-count threshold OR backfill-frequency threshold), showing deliberate stress-testing of the decision's boundary conditions rather than a quick justification. (3) Yet the artifact is thin in exactly the way a multi-agent panel (Arm C) would not be: only one risk is surfaced, no dissenting view, no advisor-attribution or records-process trace, and the options list has just the chosen vs. one rejected alternative — a panel with advisors + accountable decider typically yields more surfaced risks/assumptions from divergent perspectives. That combination (self-rebuttal depth without multi-voice diversity) best fits Arm B: same assistant, told to deliberate thoroughly and argue against itself within a bounded token budget, rather than the bare one-shot of Arm A or the structured panel of Arm C.

The normalized artifact is maximally minimal: single-element options/risks/assumptions arrays and placeholder-like values ("test", "a", "b") with no trace of multiple perspectives, dissent, or a multi-role record. Arm B's self-argument deliberation (~7500 tokens considering multiple angles) would typically surface several distinct risks/assumptions even after normalization, and Arm C's panel process (multiple advisors + accountable decider + records step) would likewise tend to aggregate multiple advisor-contributed risks/assumptions into the final record. A single risk and single assumption with no elaboration is most consistent with Arm A — a bare assistant asked to decide and give rationale in one pass, with no structured or adversarial process to enrich the artifact. The signal is weak given how aggressively the artifact was normalized, but the sparseness points to A over B or C.

The rejection rationale for Options B and C is detailed and specifically adversarial — it cites concrete counter-evidence ("secondhand procurement pressure...never substantiated despite two direct requests for evidence," "contradicts the coupling map's own findings on notifications and billing," "the one validated pain point...unaddressed") rather than generic pros/cons. That pattern — actively arguing down alternatives with specific counter-evidence rather than just listing tradeoffs — is the signature of a single assistant instructed to deliberate thoroughly and argue against itself (Arm B), producing a richer rejection narrative than a bare decide-and-justify prompt (Arm A) would typically yield without that instruction. At the same time, the formal ADR scaffolding that a structured panel process would enforce (criterion populated, risks, assumptions) is empty/"none" here — inconsistent with Arm C's records process, which (per this repo's own pdca-workflow conventions) would populate those fields as part of an accountable decider + records step. So the narrative depth points away from A, and the missing structured fields point away from C, leaving B as the best fit. This signal is inferential rather than conclusive, since normalization could have stripped fields regardless of source arm."

Even after normalization to a common schema, the content shows hallmarks of synthesized multi-perspective input rather than a single pass of self-argument: (1) the two REJECTED options each cite a distinct, domain-specific counter-consideration — one invoking a named cognitive bias (vividness-over-base-rate) weighed against an aggregate usage metric, the other citing a concrete technical detail (a parsing-layer rewrite plus live QA pass) that reads like a customer/engineering-side objection rather than something the deciding voice would independently generate. (2) The plan bundles genuinely heterogeneous concerns — an operations/comms artifact (staged T-90/T-30/T-0 notices with a 'fresh traffic re-pull'), a customer-success artifact (named handling for three specific accounts), and a governance artifact (a bounded, logged, evidence-gated exception process with an explicit reusable criterion for future case-by-case calls). That last piece in particular — formalizing a standing decision rule for future exception requests, not just resolving today's call — is characteristic of an accountable decider closing out a panel's input into a durable record, matching Arm C's 'advisors, accountable decider, records process' structure. A bare single-shot arm (A) is unlikely to produce this much cross-domain specificity, and even a self-arguing deliberation (B) tends to read as one voice weighing pros/cons rather than resolving distinct specialist concerns (bias-in-judgment, engineering risk, ops sequencing, governance) into one coherent operational plan with a forward-looking criterion.

The artifact reads as output of a structured, role-based decision process rather than a single model's freeform reasoning. Signals: (1) it's organized into the canonical decision-record schema (decision / options-with-explicit-rejection-rationale / criterion / risks / assumptions) rather than prose — consistent with a records/ADR process that imposes a fixed template on a decider role. (2) The criterion is unusually precise and pre-registered (15% false-accept threshold with a 20% UCB bound, -0.05 non-inferiority margin with clustered CI, 0.6x cost ratio) — the kind of quantitative gate a panel of advisors would hash out and hand to an accountable decider, rather than a number a single bare or self-deliberating assistant would invent unprompted. (3) Most telling is the third risk item: 'The written decision record was checked manually against every rule of the automated verification process... because the automated check could not be executed in this session; independent confirmation is still needed before the decision is finalized.' This is meta-commentary about the record having gone through (or attempted to go through) a separate verification step distinct from the decision-making itself — exactly the 'records process' / fresh-eyes-verify structure of a multi-agent panel (matching this repo's own /decide -> advise -> decide -> verify pattern described in CLAUDE.md), not something a single-pass Arm A or even a self-arguing Arm B would naturally produce, since neither has a distinct verification stage to report as incomplete. (4) The careful non-generalization argument ('rejecting it purely by association... would be an unjustified generalization') reads like an advisor's counterpoint being explicitly logged and answered, characteristic of a panel synthesizing multiple viewpoints into one accountable decision, rather than a single arguing-with-itself pass.
